name: James Whitfield
chief_complaint: My foot has gotten real bad. There's a sore on the bottom that won't heal and now it smells terrible.
onset_description: Patient has had a chronic foot ulcer on the plantar surface of his right foot for 6 weeks that he has been managing at home with over-the-counter bandages. Over the past week, the wound has become increasingly painful, started draining foul-smelling yellowish fluid, and the redness has spread up to his ankle.

foot_wound.description: There's this sore on the bottom of my foot that I got from a new pair of shoes. I've been putting bandages on it but it just keeps getting worse. Now there's this nasty yellow stuff coming out of it.
foot_wound.onset: 6 weeks ago, acutely worsened over past 5-7 days
foot_wound.character: Full-thickness ulcer approximately 3cm x 2cm on plantar surface, necrotic base with purulent drainage, surrounding erythema and induration extending to dorsum of foot and ankle
foot_wound.location: plantar surface of right foot at first metatarsal head
foot_wound.radiation: redness and swelling spreading to dorsum of foot and ankle

foot_swelling.description: My whole foot is puffed up like a balloon. Can't even get my shoe on anymore.
foot_swelling.character: diffuse swelling of entire right foot extending to ankle

fever.description: I've been feeling hot and cold the past two days. My wife made me take my temperature and it was almost 102.

decreased_sensation_feet.description: I can't really feel the bottom of my feet very well. Been that way for years. That's probably why I didn't notice the sore getting bad.
decreased_sensation_feet.character: decreased sensation to light touch, pinprick, and temperature in stocking distribution bilaterally

malaise.description: I've been feeling run down and tired for the last few days. Just not myself.

symptoms_absent: recent trauma other than shoe friction

labs.BMP: Na 135, K 4.6, Cr 1.6 (mildly elevated), BUN 28, Glucose 342 (markedly elevated)
labs.wound_culture: Pending (polymicrobial on gram stain: gram-positive cocci, gram-negative rods, anaerobes suspected)

past_medical_history: Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus (18 years, poorly controlled)
Previous diabetic foot ulcer, left foot (healed 2023)
surgical_history: Left great toe amputation (2022, diabetic osteomyelitis)
medications: Insulin lispro sliding scale before meals

social_history.smoking: Quit 3 years ago after heart stent, smoked 1.5 packs/day for 35 years (52.5 pack-year history)
social_history.living_situation: Lives with wife in a two-story home. Bedroom is upstairs. Wife helps with medication management.
social_history.exercise: Very limited due to foot pain and neuropathy. Mostly sedentary.
social_history.diet: Wife cooks most meals, tries to follow diabetic diet but admits to frequently eating sweets and large portions. Skips insulin doses occasionally.

conditions.mother: Type 2 Diabetes with bilateral below-knee amputations, died at 71 from renal failure

personality: Quiet and resigned. Feels guilty about not taking better care of himself. Knows he should have come in sooner. Worried about losing his foot like his mother did. Becomes emotional when discussing amputation risk.
communication_style: Soft-spoken and thoughtful. Gives honest answers but needs time to formulate responses. Won't volunteer information unless directly asked. Becomes withdrawn when anxious. Wife often fills in details when present.
pain_description_style: Due to neuropathy, describes pain differently than expected. Notes 'pressure' and 'throbbing' more than sharp pain. Rates pain by functional impact rather than sensation. Says 'the pain isn't as bad as it looks' because of decreased sensation.

critical_findings: Diabetic foot infection with systemic signs (fever, leukocytosis, elevated inflammatory markers)
Severely uncontrolled diabetes (HbA1c 11.2%, glucose 342) impairing wound healing
Peripheral arterial disease (ABI 0.6) compromising perfusion to affected extremity
History of contralateral toe amputation for osteomyelitis (high risk for recurrence)
Low albumin indicating poor nutritional status and impaired healing
Peripheral neuropathy masking severity of infection
Chronic kidney disease limiting contrast studies and certain antibiotics
rubric.diagnosis: Diabetic foot infection (deep soft tissue) with possible osteomyelitis
differential_diagnoses: Septic arthritis of first MTP joint
Peripheral arterial disease with ischemic ulcer and secondary infection
recommended_interventions: IV broad-spectrum antibiotics covering gram-positives, gram-negatives, and anaerobes (e.g., vancomycin + piperacillin-tazobactam)
Urgent surgical/podiatry consult for wound assessment, possible debridement
X-ray of right foot to evaluate for osteomyelitis and gas in tissues
MRI of right foot if osteomyelitis suspected (preferred over bone scan)
Tight glycemic control with IV insulin drip, hold metformin
Vascular surgery consult given low ABI for revascularization assessment
Social work consult for home support and adherence barriers
